"Effective in the first quarter of fiscal 2026, we will combine our prior segments — Digital Media, Digital Experience and Publishing and Advertising — into a single operating and reportable segment due to changes in how management intends to evaluate results, allocate resources and execute the strategic opportunities outlined above."

I think this may be due to competitive pressure. In fact, I believe it may come from Salesforce’s (CRM) data and AI platform (Agentforce and Data 360). As of January 2025, it had an ARR of $900M, reaching nearly $1.4B in October (+114% YoY), although it is true that from that point on, CRM completed a closed integration in November 2025 for $9.6B, which explains the jump seen in the reported $2.9B ARR.

For me, this is a relevant piece of evidence to include in my hypotheses, as CRM seems to be accelerating in the enterprise data business, which is exactly where ADBE is starting to decelerate.

This is also reflected by Gartner in its 2025 Magic Quadrant for Customer Data Platforms, where ADBE moves from Leader to Visionary, highlighting concerns around the “limited set of packaged integrations” and Adobe’s “platform packaging.” In other words, my interpretation is that its CDP requires the purchase of multiple adjacent products to access certain functionalities, whereas in CRM these are native. Therefore, ADBE may sell a solution that works well within its ecosystem but performs worse outside of it, allowing Salesforce to exploit this weakness in enterprise deals, as it offers a fully integrated CRM-CDP-AI stack.

With this context, the acquisition of Semrush for $1.9B makes more sense, as it provides search intelligence and optimization capabilities to gain presence in LLMs, something that would not be organically achievable with Adobe Analytics. Therefore, I consider it a coherent strategic move, but one that also reinforces my view that DX may be under pressure or losing ground, rather than simply going through a natural maturation phase.

What the Price is Telling Us

Price is a fundamental part of my analysis, and the truth is that ADBE’s structure still indicates that it is immersed in a bearish structure, not a temporary correction. On a weekly basis, the price is clearly trading below both the 30 and 200 moving averages, both with a negative slope, showing the trend that started in January 2024.

The weekly RSI around 28 indicates a possible oversold zone, but on the daily timeframe the indicator shows 43, so we cannot say that this bearish momentum is exhausted in the short term.

But the most relevant part for me comes from the volume profile, as for me this indicator is part of behavioral economics, and as we all know, market participants are not robots but people, so psychological forces come into play that we cannot ignore in our analysis. If we look closely, $234 coincides with pre-pandemic levels, with very significant trading volume before the rally to all-time highs.

Below that zone, there is a technical void that indicates that further down there is no range where the market has traded with sufficient volume, and there would be no relevant support to absorb potential selling pressure if current levels break.

At these levels and with the current market situation, I believe we are not yet at an optimal entry point from a technical perspective while the price does not signal a trend reversal. However, it does require monitoring that it stabilizes at these levels, with decreasing daily trading volume, as that would be a sign that selling pressure is fading and a bottom could begin to form from which to restart.

But for me, this situation has not yet occurred, and the price tells me that there are not enough reasons to consider an entry into the stock.

My Takeaway

To conclude, and to remain consistent with what I have laid out throughout the article, my view is to exercise extreme caution with ADBE at these levels, as I believe the market is pricing in risks that are starting to show up in the data.

It is true that Creative Cloud, which represents 58% within the 74% of the sales mix, is still holding up, but the market may be penalizing a potential future scenario of TAM compression if AI ends up reducing the number of new creatives entering the market and eventually becoming subscribers. It is not a current risk in the numbers, but it is a hypothesis to monitor.

What is verifiable, however, is that there is a part of deceleration in Digital Experience, given its growth rate, as well as the loss of leadership highlighted by Gartner. Additionally, it must be considered that CRM is accelerating in the same space, and when adding these factors together, it may be difficult to ignore that part of the sales mix could be at risk.

And the price seems to be confirming these hypotheses if we look at the current structure, with a daily RSI that does not show exhaustion and a critical trading level where, below this point, there is no relevant support almost until $150 per share.

To change my stance, a CEO with a credible AI profile would need to be appointed, and the price would also need to start stabilizing at these levels, as the fundamentals, with $10B in FCF and 89% gross margins, are strong enough to withstand pressure. But this is not a catalyst, and without one, a cheap multiple can remain cheap longer than our patience as investors might allow.

data.title: Consumer Confidence Inched Up Again In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index inched up again in March, rising 0.8 points to 91.8. The latest reading was higher than the forecast of 87.8 and is the highest level for the index so far this year.

The Present Situation Index, which is based on consumers' assessment of current business and labor market conditions, rose 4.6 points to 123.3. Meanwhile, the Expectations Index, which is based on consumers' short-term outlook for income, business, and labor market conditions, fell 1.7 points to 70.9. Note that a level of 80 or below for the Expectations Index historically signals a recession within the next year, and the index has been below 80 since February 2025.

“Consumer confidence ticked up again in March, as a modest improvement in consumers’ views of current conditions outweighed a slight downshift in expectations for the future,” said Dana M Peterson, Chief Economist, The Conference Board. “Three of five components of the Index firmed in March, and overall confidence improved modestly for a second month. Nonetheless, the Index has been on a general downward trend since 2021.”

Peterson added: “Consumers’ write-in responses on factors affecting the economy continued to skew towards pessimism. Comments about prices and the cost of goods suggest that the cost of living remained at the top of consumers’ minds. As the war in Iran overlapped significantly with the survey sample period, comments about oil/gas and war/conflict spiked, while specific mentions of trade and tariffs decreased notably.”

Unsurprisingly given the Iran war oil shock, consumers’ average and median 12-month inflation expectations surged in March to levels last seen in August 2025, when US consumers awaited more tariff announcements from the US federal government. Consequently, the percentage of consumers stating that interest rates over the next 12 months will be higher on net skyrocketed from 34.9% to 42.4%. Expectations for higher stock prices a year from now plunged.

Read more

Background on the Consumer Confidence Index

The Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index measures the consumers' attitudes and confidence in the economy, business conditions, and labor market, with higher readings indicating higher optimism. The general assumption is that when consumers are more optimistic, they will spend more and stimulate economic growth.

However, if consumers are pessimistic, then spending will decline and the economy may slow down. The index is based on a 5-question survey, with 2 questions related to present conditions and 3 questions related to future expectations. The survey began in 1967 and was conducted every two months but changed to monthly reporting in 1977, which is where our data begins.

The next two charts are attempts to evaluate the historical context for this index as a coincident indicator of the economy. In this chart I have highlighted recessions and the level the index was at at the start of each recession. The average of the consumer confidence index at the start of recessions is 101.9, a level we have been below since February 2025.

In this next chart I have included an overlay with the GDP. It is easy to see the dips in consumer confidence when GDP is negative.

The next chart includes regression lines through the consumer confidence index and through the GDP series. Interestingly, the GDP regression has a slightly negative slope, while consumer confidence has been increasing over the same time frame (since the late 1970s). It's clear that consumer confidence begins falling shortly before official recession calls.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, let's take a look at the correlation between consumer confidence and small business sentiment, the latter by way of the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index. The consumer measure is the more volatile of the two, so it is plotted on a separate axis to give a better comparison of the two series from the common baseline of 100.

As the chart illustrates, the two have tracked one another fairly closely since the onset of the financial crisis. The two have diverged at brief periods and been highly correlated at others.

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sysco: Buying Growth, Margin, And Risk
data.description:
Assume that your really major opportunities in life are going to be few, and when you get a lollapalooza, for God's sake, don't hang by like a timid little rabbit. Don't hang back. There aren't that many of the really big good ones. - Charlie Munger.

Of the companies we follow, Sysco Corporation (SYY) has probably the best long-term track record compounding value. Sysco has raised its dividend for 57 consecutive years and delivered attractive total returns for investors for decades. The last time we covered the company, we made the case that despite weakness in the restaurant sector, shares appeared undervalued.

The stock was performing well until two things happened: the conflict in the Middle East and the announcement of a major acquisition. These two events wiped out the gains that had accumulated over the past year. At first we thought it was one of those value-destroying mega acquisitions. After listening to the call and looking at the details of the transaction, it is clear that the deal has several appealing characteristics.

Management is making a bold, sizable bet that adds some risk. However, it is probably less risky than it appears at first glance. The upside is also meaningful, and we like that it is projected to be accretive in the first year. Despite the increased leverage and balance sheet risk, we have to credit management for seizing the opportunity and not being "timid rabbits," as Charlie Munger would say.

Acquisition Overview

In some ways the Restaurant Depot acquisition resembles those made by Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B). There was no sales auction. Instead, the family that owns it decided to sell, and Sysco was a good fit. As Sysco's CEO Kevin Hourican explained, "They came to Sysco because they view us as the best place to care for their family business for decades to come."

Another similarity is that the current management will stay in place. Restaurant Depot will continue operating as a separate business, even if they plan to collaborate and to use their increased scale to negotiate better prices with suppliers. Restaurant Depot also appears to have a wide moat as a low-cost operator. According to Sysco's management, the prices at Restaurant Depot can be 15% to 20% lower compared to food distributors. Despite the lower prices, Restaurant Depot has enviable margins, higher than Sysco's.

It is important to understand that Sysco is effectively buying access to a different distribution channel. While Restaurant Depot offers lower prices, customers have to drive, select, and pack the items themselves. Many small restaurant owners are willing to spend the time to get these savings. Sysco, on the other hand, currently focuses on much larger customers such as cafeterias, stadiums, hotels, and hospitals. They have scheduled deliveries and even put in storage the products for the customers. They also assign representatives to customers in case there are any issues or to provide guidance regarding which products to purchase. Management also noted that as restaurants grow, they might find it attractive to delegate the food logistics to Sysco. This way, Restaurant Depot could become a feeder channel for future customers.

Restaurant Depot has also proven to be a resilient business. One statistic that stood out is that Restaurant Depot has grown adjusted EBITDA in each of the last 30 years. This period included the global financial crisis and the pandemic. How did they manage this level of consistency? There appear to be a few key drivers. First, they are very careful in choosing the right locations when expanding. According to the acquisition presentation, all of their stores are profitable. They also own roughly 80% of their real estate. Many of these properties are strategically located in supply-constrained places that are physically close to many customers.

Restaurant Depot also appears to follow the Costco (COST) approach of searching for efficiencies and cost reductions and passing the savings to customers. This is one reason they have been able to grow even during downturns. When restaurants face a tough economy, they are more likely to search for ways to lower supply costs. During the call, Kevin Hourican even compared Restaurant Depot to Costco:

We all know Costco. They're the best run retailer in the world in my humble opinion. That is for consumers. This is the Costco of restaurants, one-stop shop.

Financial Impact

The acquisition price was not exactly low, at roughly 14.6x Restaurant Depot's adjusted operating income. However, it still looks reasonable for the quality of the business. When factoring in the expected cost synergies, the price was closer to 13x. More importantly, Restaurant Depot appears to be an even more resilient business than Sysco. It has higher profit margins and has performed better during downturns. Its large real estate holdings also add safety and a cost structure advantage versus rivals.

The trade-offs are real, though. Sysco will pause its share repurchases until it reduces the increased leverage. The acquisition is expected to stretch the balance sheet. Leverage is estimated to go up to close to 4.5x debt to adjusted EBITDA. This likely means dividend increases will probably be modest and that share buybacks will be paused until Sysco returns to below its 2.75x leverage target.

Sysco did commit to maintaining its dividend aristocrat status, which suggests it will keep raising the dividend even if by modest amounts. The acquisition is expected to be immediately accretive to earnings per share (EPS). The expected accretion was guided to be a boost in the mid- to high single digits to the adjusted EPS in the first year and low to mid-teens in the second year. Only cost synergies are modeled, despite some potential revenue synergies through cross-selling. One example of potential revenue synergies given is Sysco helping Restaurant Depot increase its private label offering.

Shareholder Returns

Sysco's dividend growth has been impressive, and it is one reason investors can rarely buy shares at a high starting yield. The recent decline has pushed the yield above 3%.

Future Outlook

Sysco itself continues to show renewed growth momentum, recovering after a weaker fiscal year 2025. Management reiterated FY2026 guidance of 3% to 5% sales growth and an adjusted EPS in the range of $4.5 to $4.6. With shares trading close to $70 at the moment, that puts the forward Price/Earnings ratio slightly above 15x. This multiple appears undemanding, particularly at a time when investors are looking for companies insulated from AI disruption. Sysco has a strong moat with its difficult-to-replicate logistics distribution network, customer relationships, and scale advantages.

Looking further ahead, once the Restaurant Depot acquisition closes, EPS could get a meaningful boost. The roughly $250 million in cost synergies are expected within the first three years. As the companies plan to continue operating separately, restructuring charges are expected to be minimal. This should help EPS growth. The biggest concern we see is the stretching of the balance sheet. The immediate impact is the pausing of the share repurchase program and probably more modest dividend increases until leverage is brought back below the company's target. Sysco's credit ratings are also at risk, with S&P Global (SPGI) already reaffirming Sysco's "BBB" rating but changing the outlook to negative from stable. One reason for optimism is that Restaurant Depot is likely to remain profitable even during a difficult economic environment given its high margins.

Valuation

Sysco is currently trading at a Price/Earnings multiple below its closest peers. Both US Foods (USFD) and The Chef's Warehouse (CHEF) are significantly more expensive. This time we are including Costco, given that there will be more overlap after the Restaurant Depot purchase and because Sysco's management sees this acquisition as the "Costco for restaurants."

Based on average analyst EPS estimates, Sysco looks very inexpensive. For example, the forward FY2028 Price/Earnings is slightly above 12x. For a high-quality business with the competitive advantages and track record that Sysco has, we see these multiples as undervaluing the company. Based on the expected earnings accretion from the acquisition of Restaurant Depot, we expect analysts to revise their estimates higher.

Risks

Sysco has mitigated several of the risks that are typical in large acquisitions by keeping the company as a separate business. It will be run by the current management, which should maintain the current company culture. Restaurant Depot's existing shareholders will own a significant percentage of Sysco post-merger, further aligning incentives. There is some regulatory approval risk. However, given that Sysco and Restaurant Depot operate in different channels, this risk is relatively low.

The biggest threat we see is a credit rating downgrade if leverage increases more than expected. S&P Global has already given a warning. The earnings boost from the acquisition could also be under threat if interest rates are meaningfully higher by the time the acquisition closes and Sysco issues the debt and hybrid bonds.

Takeaway

Sysco's management should be given credit for seizing the opportunity to make a bold acquisition. The fact that the company that owns Restaurant Depot decided to sell directly to Sysco, instead of auctioning the business, shows the value of having a good reputation in the industry. Integration and operational risks are low, as Restaurant Depot will continue operating as a separate business with the current management remaining in place. The cost synergies appear realistic and could surprise to the upside.

It is rare to see a major acquisition guided to be immediately accretive to margins and earnings per share. There are some drawbacks, including a pause to share repurchases. Dividend growth will likely be modest for the next few years, and stretching the balance sheet adds risks. Despite the added risks, the potential benefits are substantial. The price drop after the announcement looks like an overreaction.

data.title: ASHR: Paying A Premium For Great Companies May Be Worth It
data.description:
It has been a while since I last covered Chinese ETFs, so it's time for an update. A year ago, I published my initial analysis on the Xtrackers Harvest CSI 300 China A-Shares ETF (ASHR), which was titled "Betting On The Chinese Economy At A Time Most Investors Are Fearful."

Buying into the fear was rewarded, as the ETF achieved a 22.62% total return in a year. A great result for a market that was widely labeled as "uninvestable."

While outperformance is great, the earnings of the companies in the ETF also grew in the double digits. In other words, the fundamentals caught up with the share price appreciation, and there's no reason for me to turn less bullish. I keep my "Buy" rating.

Also, the valuation gap between H-shares and A-shares has narrowed significantly since its peak a couple of years ago, which makes A-share ETFs even more attractive.

In this article, we're going to tune out the short-term noise and focus on the fundamentals of the underlying companies this ETF holds. As a long-term investor, I tend to focus on the long term. If you're interested in short-term predictions, this article might not be for you.

Holdings Breakdown

ASHR has 286 holdings, and the top ten holdings represent 22.75% of the assets. It's not overly concentrated, and I view this diversification as a positive when we're investing in emerging markets that tend to be quite volatile.

The sector breakdown also looks good, as no sector represents more than 24% of the assets.

I put special emphasis on the turnover rate; it shows how often an ETF rebalances its holdings. As long-term investors, we want to see a low turnover rate, as a high rate leads to unnecessary transaction fees.

ASHR's turnover rate is 143%. It's well above my comfort zone at this point, and it's a negative we should keep in mind.

A-Shares Vs. H-Shares

ASHR only holds A-shares, and for companies with dual listings, H-shares historically have been cheaper. There has been a wide valuation gap, but it's narrowing.

When it comes to Chinese equities, we have to differentiate between H-shares and A-shares. A-shares are traded in Mainland China and are widely available to Chinese retail investors. H-shares are traded in Hong Kong, and they're not as easily accessible to investors in China. Therefore, the valuation of H-shares depends on the sentiment of foreign investors, and they tend to trade at a discount compared to A-shares.

The gap was especially wide when I invested in Chinese companies at the peak of pessimism a couple of years ago. So, where do we stand now?

Out of ASHR's top ten holdings, four companies have a dual listing in Hong Kong and Mainland China. Let's quickly compare the P/Es and dividend yields of the A-shares and H-shares that ASHR holds.

Company: Ping An Insurance Zijin Mining Group China Merchants Bank Midea Group
A-Share P/E Ratio: 8.69 25.18 6.83 13.53
H-Share P/E Ratio: 7.90 23.56 7.33 13.04
A-Share Dividend Yield: 4.14% 1.56% 4.98% 5.22%
H-Share Dividend Yield: 4.55% 1.67% 4.63% 5.40%

(Own calculations based on the companies' annual reports)

The four companies demand a 13.55x P/E for their A-shares on average and a 12.96x P/E for the H-shares. That's a 5% premium for essentially the same shares. We receive the same dividends and rights, so in theory, it doesn't make sense to pay up for the A-shares.

Since I first covered Chinese ETFs, the gap has narrowed substantially, but not surprisingly, there is still a gap. Lower dividend yields and higher starting valuations equal higher risks and lower returns. So, I'm still not a fan of holding A-shares whenever a cheaper dual listing exists, but it's one of the downsides of holding an ETF like ASHR that we have to accept.

You won't find an ETF that only holds A-shares without dual listings. And it's very difficult for foreign investors to invest in individual A-shares, so we have to go the ETF route.

On the positive side, it's encouraging for new investors in ASHR that the gap has narrowed, and we're not paying the huge premium that we paid two years ago when the pessimism was at its peak.

Investing in A-share ETFs can still make sense, because many of the most promising companies in China are only listed in Mainland China and not in Hong Kong.

P/E Ratio And Growth Rates

If we're investing in stocks (which equals owning parts of businesses), there are two important considerations. First, we don't want to overpay. Second, the higher the valuation we pay, the higher the future growth has to be in order to justify that valuation.

Therefore, let's quickly go through the P/Es and historical growth rates of ASHR's top holdings. Blindly relying on third-party data when it comes to the valuation of any ETF can be confusing. For example, Yahoo Finance has the P/E at 15.76, while the Wall Street Journal (WSJ) claims it's 27.93. That's a steep difference, so who is right?

Arithmetic Average Vs. Harmonic Mean

And the answer is both are right in their own way. The WSJ calculates the arithmetic average. For example, if an ETF holds one stock with a P/E of 40 and another one with a P/E of 10, the arithmetic average comes down to 25. We just add 10 to 40 and divide it by 2.

Other providers like Yahoo Finance use the harmonic mean. If we have the same two companies and invest $1,000 in each, we receive $125 in earnings. The 40x P/E company gives us $25 in earnings, and the 10x P/E company $100 in earnings. In total, we have invested $2,000 and received $125 in earnings for a combined P/E of 16. Quite a difference compared to the arithmetic average that the WSJ uses.

I have to admit, in the past, I was quite confused about the different calculation methods. By doing my own arithmetic average calculations, I always sided with the WSJ and used their numbers for reference. Now it finally made "click", and I believe the harmonic average used by Yahoo Finance and other providers also makes sense.

Calculating The Top Holding's Valuation

Let's quickly calculate the arithmetic average and harmonic mean ourselves by going through the top holdings. We're also going to focus on past growth rates to get a hint of which type of companies the ETF invests in.

Here's a list of ASHR's top twenty holdings and their P/Es and historical growth rates:

Company: P/E Ratio: Annual EPS Growth Rate (Past 5 Years):
Contemporary Amperex Technology 24.91 62.80%
Kweichow Moutai 19.62 16.10%
Zhongji Innolight 78.38 52.30%
Ping An Insurance Group 8.69 -7.40%
Zijin Mining Group 25.18 44.50%
China Merchants Bank 6.83 10.80%
Eoptlink Technology 60.85 48.70%
Midea Group 13.53 9.10%
China Yangtze Power 20.01 4.10%
Industrial Bank 5.68 1.00%
BYD 23.87 80.90%
CITIC Securities 12.00 5.90%
Sungrow Power Supply 22.84 67.00%
Luxshare Precision 21.34 20.70%
Jiangsu Hengrui 45.50 -0.50%
ICBC 5.95 -1.80%
Wuxi AppTec 17.38 44.30%
Cambricon Technologies 199.04 Nil
NAURA Technology 51.07 139.10%
Hygon Information Tech 202.98 Nil

(Own calculations & Gurufocus.com)

The twenty companies above represent 34% of ASHR's total assets. So, keep in mind that the following calculations don't fully represent the whole ETF's P/E ratio and growth rates, but we get a fair estimate.

I quickly scanned through the cash flow statements over the last decade, and the average quality of the companies is decent. Most companies convert their earnings into free cash flow, which is great.

We have some stagnant companies like the Chinese banks, insurance companies, or energy firms, but they come with a single-digit P/E and high dividend yields. Other than that, most companies saw great double-digit growth over the past five years.

If we use the arithmetic average, the average P/E ratio for the stocks listed above is 42.38. If we exclude the companies with triple-digit earnings multiples that distort the averages, we arrive at a 23.18x P/E. So, it seems like the WSJ numbers are fairly accurate once again.

I calculated the harmonic mean, and it comes down to 16.46. It's roughly in line with the numbers provided by Yahoo Finance. So, for every $16.46 we invest in ASHR, we receive $1 in earnings. But the average company in the ETF is trading at a 27x P/E ratio, which makes it quite vulnerable if the sentiment for Chinese equities were to sour.

The average company grew its EPS by 33.20% annually over the past five years. Pretty impressive! But I wouldn't expect future growth rates to mirror past growth rates, because some of the holdings are quite cyclical and may be near their cycle peaks. Zijin Mining Group, or the electric vehicle and battery manufacturers, for example. Also, the top holdings are mostly the winners of the past, and those tend to come with higher past growth rates.

Some analysts expect a 15% increase in MSCI China A-share net profits in 2026. I believe this estimate is decent.

Despite the constant negative headlines around the country, China has managed to build up some of the most impressive companies globally in almost every sector. These are companies the average investor may have never heard of, yet they're growing at a rapid rate and gaining market share.

Who's Laughing Now?

And you don't have to be an investor in Chinese shares to spot what has been going on. The rapid rise of China is one of the main reasons why Western countries like Germany are struggling at the moment. The Chinese have largely caught up and, in many instances, are already ahead when it comes to future technologies.

Historically, Western companies could demand a premium price for their goods, but if the Chinese companies can produce similar (or even better) quality at lower prices, who's going to pay that premium?

I still remember the sentiment around Chinese cars twenty years ago. At that time, the Chinese tried to enter the German market. The cars were bad, like really awful. At that time, German car manufacturers were laughing at the Chinese competition (so did Elon Musk), and now, twenty years later, they're not laughing any longer. In my view, it's an existential threat to Europe's industrial business model.

I believe that long-term investors shouldn't dismiss Chinese equities. Sure, there are geopolitical risks. One has to consider position sizes and risk management. It's crucial to find a good balance between risk and reward. However, to have zero exposure to the country, at least to me, seems risky over the long term.

ASHR offers exposure to some of the best and fastest-growing companies in the country. The valuation looks steep to a value investor like me, but if we factor in the growth rates, it still looks like a decent deal.

Conclusion

When I first wrote about Chinese ETFs, I highlighted the technological advancement of the country and the low valuations at that time. Despite the negative headlines, the country is progressing rapidly and is attacking almost every sector where Western companies have held a technological advantage for decades. If you ask me, it will continue.

So, despite the geopolitical risks, I view it as risky for long-term global investors to shun the country. That said, maintaining conservative position sizes and risk management is crucial.

With an ETF like ASHR, we're gaining access to some of the best companies the country has to offer. The valuation isn't cheap, but given the expected growth rates, it may be worth it. It remains a cautious "Buy" for patient long-term investors who are comfortable with the volatility, the high turnover rate, and the risks involved.

data.title: TG Therapeutics: Briumvi Performance Aiding Upside, Buybacks Limiting Downside
data.description:
TG Therapeutics' (TGTX) multiple sclerosis (MS) drug, Briumvi, appears on the way to blockbuster status with net revenues nearing $600M in 2025, just three years into its launch. I rated TGTX a buy in November 2025, noting clinical work with Briumvi now could help drive revenue growth in the future, and a media campaign could deliver revenue growth near-term. This article looks at the performance of Briumvi since that article, updates from the company's clinical work, and the company's financials. With commercial performance remaining strong, a catalyst near-term with results from the ENHANCE trial and stock buybacks helping to support the stock, I again rate TGTX a buy.

Briumvi Growth Isn't Petering Out

Despite my buy rating on TGTX in November, in August, following Q2'25 earnings, I noted percentage growth in each subsequent quarter of sales of Briumvi appeared to be slowing. Of course a drug can't keep up the 100% or 50% quarterly increases in growth that are seen earlier in launch, but I did want to keep an eye on whether the rate of growth from one quarter to the next would continue to slip, perhaps below 10%. Such a scenario would put more pressure on things like subcutaneous (SC) Briumvi or treatment of new diseases altogether to drive revenues of Briumvi markedly higher.

Q4'25 earnings, however, have come with US net product revenues of $182.7M, a 19.5% increase from the $152.9M reported in Q3'25.

Table 1: US Briumvi net product revenues by quarter. Table by Biotech Beast; numbers from company press releases and quarterly filings.

US Briumvi net product revenue ($M)

Growth Over Prior Quarter
Q1'23 7.8 -
Q2'23 16 105%
Q3'23 25.1 57%
Q4'23 39.9 59%
Q1'24 50.5 27%
Q2'24 72.6 44%
Q3'24 83.3 15%
Q4'24 103.6 24%
Q1'25 119.7 16%
Q2'25 138.8 16%
Q3'25 152.9 10%
Q4'25 182.7 19%

It appears growth of IV Briumvi is not petering out just yet, potentially aided by certain marketing efforts, such as direct-to-consumer (DTC) marketing. The company notes, however, that it is hard to name just one source of growth.

... we're seeing record new patient enrollments here. We're still seeing growth. We're seeing great share gains in the IV segment.

And I think that's being driven by the things that I mentioned, durability of the clinical profile, the 6-year data, the operational advantages, our investment in both the expansion of our field force. It could very well be our DTC efforts, although hard to single out a single factor here. But all these things, I think, are leading to continued confidence in the product across the board, and that's why we're seeing such a great demand that we're seeing right now.

Adam Waldman, CCO at TGTX, Q4'25 earnings call.

Regarding those marketing efforts, TGTX began a TV marketing campaign in Q3'25, which continued in Q4'25, with Q1'26 seeing Briumvi advertised during Super Bowl LX. TGTX's Briumvi launch featured low spending on marketing earlier in its launch. For example, note SG&A expenses rising to $62.7M in Q4'25 from $39M in Q4'24.

With 2025 seeing DTC marketing and expansion of the sales force in the field, TGTX has only really begun to push Briumvi in earnest, so while it has resulted in an increase in expenses, it may deliver revenue growth throughout 2026.

Clinical Progress

TGTX's ENHANCE study is an attempt to simplify initial dosing of IV Briumvi with the two initial doses that are normally 15 days apart consolidated into one infusion. That regime could encourage patients to start on the drug or switch to the drug because of its convenience. The rapid enrollment of ENHANCE due to patient and physician enthusiasm bodes well for the potential marketing impact of the study if it is successful and results in a simplified initial dosing of Briumvi.

Given TGTX would already have insights on how well a consolidated dosing regime of Briumvi is tolerated, I think they would likely have abandoned efforts by now if that regime caused too many issues to be viable.

The company's Q4'25 earnings have come with confirmation of an ENHANCE readout in "mid-2026," perhaps putting a late Q3'26 readout as unlikely, so perhaps by the end of August, in my mind. The company expects a 2027 launch if that trial is successful. The bigger readout might be the SC Briumvi work, which is expected to produce a result around "year-end 2026/1Q 2027," according to the Q4'25 earnings press release.

'26, we expect to be even stronger, including a number of very exciting catalysts, including the top line data from our ENHANCE trial, some preliminary data from Azer-cel and then perhaps the biggest one of all at the end of the year or early next year, pivotal data from the subcutaneous BRIUMVI program.

Michael Weiss, Chairman, CEO & President at TGTX, Q4'25 earnings call.

An SC Briumvi would allow the company to steal patients from Novartis AG's (NVS) Kesimpta (ofatumumab), which has a very convenient auto-injector. TGTX's Briumvi has very favorable pharmacokinetics, with patients being able to go for an infusion every 24 weeks following completion of initial dosing.

Moreover, the company has repeatedly made comments suggesting they should be able to successfully adapt the drug to infrequent subcutaneous dosing, perhaps every 8-12 weeks following an initial two doses 8-12 weeks apart (see the January presentation slide above for an example). I believe a successful result from the Briumvi SC pivotal trial is quite likely.

Financial Overview

TGTX finished Q4'25 with cash, cash equivalents, and investment securities of $199.5M. For Q4'25, the company reported total revenue of $192.6M, cost of revenue of $38.1M, R&D expense of $41.2M, SG&A expense of $62.7M, and a net income of $23M. The company's net cash used in operating activities was $24.8M in 2025, although revenues are ever increasing and that number might change greatly in 2026 to reflect net cash provided by operating activities.

TGTX did have $245.6M in non-current loans payable on its balance sheet at the end of Q4'25, relating to its $250M term loan with Blue Owl Capital (OWL). However, on March 19, 2026, the company reported it had entered into a new five-year, $750M senior secured credit facility with OWL. That deal involved repaying the $250M outstanding from the prior deal, which means TGTX has raised $500M in funds, without dilution, albeit by taking on more debt.

Perhaps more remarkable for those looking to trade or invest in TGTX, either long or short, is the fact that the company also announced with the new credit facility that it would increase the size of its buyback program from $100M to $300M.

As of March 18, 2026, the Company has repurchased approximately $38 million of common stock under the prior and existing share repurchase program at an average price of $28.98 per share.

Comments from the TGTX press release, March 19, 2026.

We might suggest, then, that it would be hard for the stock to trade well below the average price at which the company has bought back stock, since the company would likely step in to buy stock at that level, without some sort of major material update (see Risks section below).

TGTX trades currently with a market cap of $5B ($31.51 per share). The company has guided for total global revenue of $875M-$900M in 2026.

Risks

There are multiple risks to a long position in TGTX, a few of which are discussed in this section.

While the share price could have support from the buyback program, it is unlikely a quarter of no sales growth in Q1'26 wouldn't hit the stock. TGTX can easily trade well below $29 if the Briumvi launch stalls out.

While ENHANCE represents a near-term opportunity to announce positive data from a clinical readout, given the company's enthusiasm, an increase in side effects from consolidating two initial doses of Briumvi into one might not be well received. Investors could question how much impact the consolidated dosing regime could have if it is more convenient but comes with more side effects. The same risk of underperformance from clinical trials applies to the subcutaneous work around year-end or Q1'27, and even with updates from early-stage pipeline member azer-cel.

Lastly, TGTX could look to diversify further beyond azer-cel and Briumvi by picking up a new asset to add to the pipeline. While there are opportunities to develop Briumvi and azer-cel in other indications, allowing the company to diversify without acquiring a new drug, there is still no guarantee TGTX won't do this.

Conclusions

It looks to me as if TGTX, trading with a market cap of $5B currently, might even be able to get towards total global revenue of $1B in 2026 if its initial guidance of $875M-$900M gets soundly beaten. Most of those revenues are simply off the back of US net product revenues from Briumvi, which looks like it could achieve blockbuster status in the US alone, perhaps simply by continuing to market the current IV schedule.

Of course, if ENHANCE confirms Briumvi can be administered by a more convenient dosing schedule, that could make reaching blockbuster sales in the US alone even easier. Perhaps even if ENHANCE weren't successful, the DTC efforts and sales force expansion alone could drive Briumvi IV towards a $1B run rate in the US within a year anyway. Once you consider Briumvi SC on top of that, buybacks limiting potential downside, and even just the run-up effect that could be seen in results from the pivotal Briumvi SC trial, I think the TGTX bull case looks strong here. I currently rate TGTX a buy.

data.title: Recent AI Funding Problems Should Worry You
data.description:
It's no secret that I'm not exactly an AI or software bull in general. It's also no secret that I expect something pretty bad to happen in the market eventually, once this current capital-fueled spending spree aimed at AI and Data centers starts winding down.

In my daily investing and analyzing practice, I see many indicators of overvaluation, which have started encroaching in sectors that I did not expect it to do. From companies like Utilities, to companies like Construction and building materials companies to companies even such as insurers, many industries are seeing surges in valuation related to capital tied in turn to expectations or forecasts for AI investments.

It's not as though the bearish assumptions are unknown. However, I believe as we're now moving into 2026, and as things are starting to clear up, and debt investors and capital companies are starting to raise their eyebrows, I think the worries are going to rise slowly but surely until they at some point "pop" - much like the market popped following COVID-19 (though obviously for entirely different reasons).

So what I will do in this article is to lay out the worries I have for the AI-fueled surge of growth and spending - what I believe will "break" it and why I believe this is starting to happen even as we speak (and some of the worrying signals there).

AI Infrastructure Investments Are Massive, But So Far Mostly Unprofitable

It's no secret now that for some time, AI companies have been looking to invest, to raise funds for investing, and to scale their operations. As usual, the market seems deadlocked on two very opposing views.

One view that's positive says that not only is the current rally and growth likely to continue and justified to do so, it might also grow even further.

The other view, which is negative, is that there are so many clear signs of a bubble that anyone not seeing them would be blinding themselves.

The Bearish And Bullish Camps For AI

The first "camp" is essentially the following points.

The AI spending bubble cannot be compared to the dot-com bubble (which I have been doing in the past) because, unlike back then, the giants involved here are generating very tangible revenue. This is not just hype; it's a change in business models and strategy. The productivity boosts are not just hype either; we're likely to see double-digit savings for businesses, which will justify what's going on here.

Furthermore, the demand for AI hardware, in the form of chips and centers, is continuing to outgrow what the industry can provide. This continues to justify the significant valuations that many of these companies trade at and could even justify a higher valuation.

At the same time, the fact that all the major tech companies are so extremely well capitalized means that reliance for these companies on debt is not really there. They have the cash needed to support the current build-out. Therefore, the fundamental argument of a bubble is wrong.

The second "camp" advocates for the following counter-arguments.

The current plan of spending comes to something like $3T on AI infrastructure, and despite the well-capitalized state of many of the companies involved, about half of this is likely to be debt.

This means, even at conservative interest rates, we're talking about $75B annually in interest payments - not amortization. The so-called hyperscalers and major companies have already issued over $120B in bonds during 2025, and this in itself is an increase of over 290% from previous years. And this, as you can see above, addresses less than 10% of the debt needed for the estimated growth CapEx.

The camp further argues that despite significant capital spending and massive revenues from the hyperscalers, revenue increases (or, for that matter, cost reductions) aren't happening. Add to that that less than 5% of people even pay for AI today. The attention paid to this suggests a very fundamental problem in the calculations that might go beyond the "pilot" phase - it might be that OpEx has been misforecasted, it might be that this is not ready, and it might be other things.

There are finance-technical worries as well, in that, first, many of these loans rely not on traditional bonds and banks but on private credit financing. That standard interest rate I mentioned above is not going to be applicable to those loans—5-12% is more likely.

That would bring interest costs alone for the expected expansion upwards of $150B per year. That's just for that CapEx growth, and that is compared to a total AI revenue of about $638B in 2024.

Now, if you look at the bullish assumptions for the value of the AI market going forward, this is not a problem. Because that market is going to be over $3.8T in about 8 years. Note, however, as of right now, less than $1.6T for this expansion for 2028E is actually expended by the so-called hyperscalers.

And while these hyperscalers can, for the time being, borrow at low rates due to their cash flows, the question is what will happen when this dries up?

That, I argue, is the essence of the bearish view.

Indications Of Funding Trouble - For Those Who Watch The Markets

It's quite obvious as of late, perhaps for 3-4 months now, that credit companies are growing increasingly hesitant to fund this spending spree.

I will name some examples here. We have companies paying bond yields over 10% to get money due to AI-related risk as of late. We further have banks being unable, or the market implying a lack of availability to being able to offload loans to certain sectors, so-called "hung loans" and deals that were pulled back. We also have the by-now well-known example of Blue Owl (OWL) having to halt withdrawals due to AI-related risks, which caused a small crash in their stock price in less than a week.

I have covered Blue Owl specifically in the IG, but in essence, over 65% of OWL's portfolio is concentrated in software firms, and the impacts of Cov-lite loan structures, PIK (payment-in-kind) structures, and other risks, which I believe are effectively hollowing out underwriting standards and resulting in debt, are covered by what looks like "good safety", but in fact may not be. These loans are further worth focusing on due to significantly high leverage, very hard-to-understand valuation of the underlying assets, and similar issues.

There is a reason why banks aren't lending money to AI investments unless they are backed by one of the hyperscalers or by significant equity. It's the same reason why you would face difficulties getting funding from a bank if you were to show up with nothing but a laptop and a PowerPoint presentation - a lack of tangible collateral. IP is only as valuable, and talent is only as valuable as the physical property that underlies it - unless you prove the former is valuable. When you're starting out, that's a tough call.

Regulated financial institutions don't want to get involved in the business because they have, as the term suggests, regulators that watch over them. They have very little interest in getting involved in the sort of risk that a 10%+ bond yield in a 4% treasury environment implies.

The internet is rife with articles concerning "why everyone else fails at AI and what you need to do instead."

The problem with these articles? It seems that more than half of them are being written by AI, and the way that AI works, this becomes a self-perpetuating "bullish loop".

I pride myself on trying to keep to objective factors and facts. Some of what I mentioned above was on the subjective, or perhaps more on the speculative, side. So let's move back to what I believe the core of the problem is and why you should be worried (if you're bullish on AI).

Why The Capital/Debt Financing Doesn't Compute In 2028E

I'm going to make a small reference to the crypto market when it was still "the hype". When mining coins became popular, hardware firms quickly came out with newer and newer hardware, which increased asset depreciation because the hardware quickly became obsolete. I argue the same thing is happening with the current data center infrastructure. Much of the hardware that was deployed as little as 1-2 years ago is likely to be obsolete in 1-2 years.

By that argument, the $3T estimate in funding might actually be conservative if the bullish investors want to keep this hype going. The fact that market participants are arguing for $3T worth of CapEx into a sector where 95% of AI projects fail to deliver positive ROI is, to me, something that "doesn't work."

The AI bubble has started, easily, to spread into traditional banking, private credit, utilities, construction, and infrastructure sectors. That to me is clear. That is why I am out of many of these investments at excellent profit.

When I wrote this article, I found that it's very difficult to actually put these risks that I see into English, but I have done my best to do so here.

It's very clear that the participants are no longer funding just through equity or cash flows. They are moving into debt. This is a shift from how these tech companies operate. Net cash positions have previously meant that the sector was seen as low risk. When bond yields for many of the smaller ones are going above 10%, we're going to see a significant test of this assumption of conservatism. Debt-funded CapEx introduces not only risk, but it also raises leverage and increases rating pressure. It doesn't help that it's very unclear at this point who the winners and losers, if any, of this AI race are going to be.

The worrying part is that this is a relatively new trend. It wasn't until 4Q25, which makes it less than a full quarter, that the companies working with data centers started to move to debt.

I want to convey to you that this historical issue of debt in 2025 is not the issue to me, as odd as this may sound. This was done mostly at very low rates by companies with fortress-like balance sheets. Mostly. We're talking Meta (FB) and the like. What worries me are the signs now - massive yields and private credit companies already seeing a strain, despite less than 10% of the growth being funded.

What worries me also is that the infrastructure that's being built, to touch back on my point above, actually has no track record whatsoever. How do you estimate your RoR on "AI infrastructure"? It's currently all being done by best guesses. But how do you "guess" for projects with a 95% ROI failure rate?

What I see as worrying is the combination of these assumptions and exactly how willing market participants are to fund this growth. I'm not talking about you as a market participant buying 1,000 or even 10,000 shares of FB. Instead, put yourself in the shoes of a fund manager being asked to fund $3B in debt by a project that can't really give you concrete return figures or a timeline backed by anything aside from speculation.

Would you rather invest the money on your sheet in that or take your chance with a 4% bond yield?

Summing Up

It's very clear to me that while things may not be in panic or worry mode yet, the time when savvy investors should start worrying is when warning signs start to appear - not when they are as clear as an air-raid siren to everyone with working ears.

And while I may be exaggerating that the time is now, I am saying clearly that there are rising signs of worry here - signs that the funding for the $3T worth of debt is unlikely to be as easy as some expect. The fact that so few bullish (on AI) investors speak about things like circular funding, where major hyperscalers buy services or assets from one another, which has been proven is what is happening, shows me how willing investors can be to believe their own versions of things.

The absolutely hardest thing you can do as an investor and analyst is to take a look at your highest-conviction assumptions with the look of one on the other side of the spectrum. If you're bullish, go bearish, and vice versa. Try to argue against your own view and see if your conviction holds, and do so objectively. Better yet, get an informed contrarian to your own view to argue against you, and have a spirited debate.

That is what I did in preparation for this article - and we were willing to admit that while we didn't change our own views, I walked away a little bit more informed and a little less gloomy about the near term, and he walked away a little less convinced about the long term.

I therefore raise a bit of a finger of warning in this article and suggest that you check your assumptions.

Because signs of worry are there.

data.title: Deere Is Finally Receiving Winds Of Change
data.description:
Deere & Company (DE) is an excellent company, but its cyclicality might cause some misinterpretation of its valuation. Currently, DE is valued at a superficially read 31 times forward P/E, which might seem, of course, quite expensive. Despite this, I believe the stock will reduce that high P/E ratio rather quickly, especially in the next year, due to cyclicality finally favoring the stock's earnings. For me, the stock is a shy buy, and every pullback might be a good opportunity to buy the dip.

Additionally, it is also important to consider that Deere is an extremely important capital goods provider in the agricultural sphere, which means that any shock that could raise food and energy prices would eventually mean gains for Deere's bottom line in the short and mid-term.

Even though the stock has been stable since 2021, it has still managed to provide higher returns than the general index (SPY). This is because of good capital allocation in buybacks, dividends, and reinvesting in the business to increase margins.

Deere: The Current Opportunity

I'll try to be clear. I think Deere is a good buy at this price, not only because of its business quality but also because of the turnaround in its cyclicality, which might be enhanced by the Iran conflict.

Deere: The Business

Deere, according to its 10-K, is a widely known brand of agricultural equipment, agricultural vehicles, and even construction and forestry machines. The company also has a revenue segment related to financial services, which helps clients to get Deere's products more easily.

The company has 3 main business lines: Production and Precision Agriculture (PPA), Small Agriculture and Turf (SAT), and Construction and Forestry (CF). Usually, PPA is the bigger revenue segment, with around 45% of revenues, followed by CF and SAT, which have around 25% of total revenues each. Despite that, profitability can be all over the place for each segment. That is because of the cyclical nature of Deere, which will increase or decrease profits more accordingly with the business momentum.

For example, for Q1 2026, Deere had $139 million in operating profit for the PPA segment, $196 million for the SAT division, and $137 million in CF. Despite that, the company will see a disproportionate increase in profitability when the PPA segment's revenues grow significantly again. In terms of net sales, PPA grew 3% year over year, while SAT and CF saw a real material increase of 24% and 34%, respectively. That alone has signaled to the management in the last earnings call that a shift in the company's revenues is finally in sight. Let's remember that DE has been seeing a decrease in revenues since the end of 2023, brought on by higher-for-longer interest rates to fight the inflation seen in 2022.

Another characteristic of the business that will help Deere capitalize on this growth is that it is the market leader in the agricultural business category. This means that when the shift in this machinery demand happens, DE will be the first one to benefit and the one company that might enjoy the best margin expansion, according to scale economics. Additionally, I believe Deere has a strong brand, and proof of that is that the company is able to keep its margins positive despite dramatically lower sales in the business cycle. The company lost $17 billion in revenues since 2023 but only around $7 billion in operating profit, which, in my opinion, is impressive for an industrial company. For example, one of its competitors, CNH Industrial (CNH), has lost almost the entirety of its operating income since 2023, from almost $3 billion to the current $724 million.

Talking a bit more specifically about the forecasts, the management expects that the PPA revenues will still contract this year by 5 to 10%, while the operating margin should be around just 12%, down from 15.4% in 2025. In SAT, the management sees an increase in revenues of 15% and an operating margin expansion from 12% to 15%. In construction and forestry, the management forecasts a 15% increase in revenues and an operating margin expansion from 9 to 10%. That generates an outlook of $4.5 to $5 billion in revenues for 2026, which will mean that finally the company will see the long-awaited bottom that the business needs to reignite growth.

The Cyclicality

Now, discussing Deere's cyclicality, I think this will also mark a top in the company's valuation ratios. Currently, DE is valued at 31 times this year's earnings, which, of course, is expensive if we omit that this is still a cyclical company. For example, if Deere sees an increase in 2027 of 15% in sales in PPA while sustaining a 15% increase in SAT and CF, it would mean operating profits close to 2024 numbers in 2027 and earnings likely in the $7.5 billion region. That could cause the valuation to fall quickly to a more realistic 20 times P/E ratio at this current price, and that would be only the middle of the cycle. It is still difficult to see from here what the peak of the cycle would look like, at $9 billion profits, maybe, which might cause the valuation to fall even further to a 16 times P/E ratio.

Deere's cyclicality is also extremely tied to the North American economy and can be potentiated if, at any moment, the Federal Reserve lowers interest rates, something that might happen before the end of the decade that could strongly propel Deere's revenues.

The Conflict In The Middle East

Unfortunately, the current war in Iran has caused the Strait of Hormuz to be closed and many oil facilities to be shut down momentarily. This supply shock will very likely cause an increase in oil prices for some time, because restarting production is not an easy task; it is not as simple as reopening the valve. Instead, the process is difficult and risky, so the supply shock might last for some time. Additionally, Qatar's LNG production has been capped after Iran destroyed 17% of its export capacity. Those shocks are not quick to resolve and might keep energy prices elevated for a while after the war ends, and we are not even there.

Energy price increases would cause food prices to increase, increasing the demand for agricultural machinery and, generally, all capital goods, increasing the revenues and margins of Deere. This might work as a boost, kind of like a catalyst, not a real revenue trend, but it will still accelerate the cycle for the company.

Balance Sheet And Shareholder Returns

Deere's balance sheet is pretty good, with enough assets to cover each category: current, long-term, and the financial division. According to Fitch, Deere has an A1 credit rating, which is very nice to see.

From a shareholder's returns perspective, the company returned around 60% of the cash from operations to the shareholders from 2015 to 2024 in the form of either dividends or buybacks. DE returned just $1 billion in buybacks last year but has kept the $1.7 billion dividend afloat and intact, which is a good sign, although it represents a tiny dividend yield of 1.14% currently. The company is just ok from this perspective.

Valuation

I believe the subjacent business model of Deere is awesome, but unfortunately, it is still quite cyclical. Even though the company doesn't look that attractive at the current P/E, it is still worth remembering that as the cycle stabilizes and goes to the peak, the P/E will contract severely.

For that reason, I will use a 24 times P/E ratio as a terminal value in the following model. Additionally, I would project earnings growing at a decent pace until 2030, since the cycle is coming back. I also consider a decrease of 8 million shares year over year.

Regarding the model, Deere can generate a 15% CAGR, without counting dividends, which is pretty decent compared to the average 8% that the SP500 might give on average.

Risks

Deere is an excellent company, but it is still far from immune to different issues. For example, the current private credit problems could cause a credit freeze if everything gets out of control in the financial economy. This alone could severely impact capital goods stocks like DE. Other macroeconomic worries or significant disruptions to supply chains could also inflict significant losses on shareholders from this point.

Takeaway

Deere is an awesome business, with a solid track record and shareholder returns. Even though I would like lower prices to buy the stock, it is still at this point a good investment for the next few years, as the company's cycle improves and goes from bottom to peak. I think it is worth it to begin nibbling at the stock here and there to eventually make a consolidated position in a very well-diversified portfolio.
